data.title: U.S. Senate Uncovers Evidence of Operation Chokepoint 2.0
data.description: According to Odaily, U.S. Senator Cynthia Lummis announced that the Senate Banking Committee has discovered the first concrete evidence of Operation Chokepoint 2.0. She assured that the Digital Assets Subcommittee is committed to identifying the involved parties and holding them accountable.

data.title: Musk's opinion poll shows more than 90% of participants support the government efficiency department to audit the US Internal Revenue Service
data.description: Odaily Planet Daily News: The X poll initiated by Musk asking the Department of Government Efficiency (DOGE) whether the U.S. Internal Revenue Service should be audited has ended, and ultimately received nearly 1.9 million votes, with 92% of participants ultimately in favor.

data.title: Musk's Poll Suggests Audit Of IRS Gains Strong Support
data.description: According to Odaily, a poll initiated by Elon Musk regarding whether the Department of Government Efficiency (DOGE) should audit the U.S. Internal Revenue Service (IRS) has concluded. The poll received nearly 1.9 million votes, with 92% of participants expressing support for the audit.

data.title: Senator Lummis: Digital Assets Subcommittee Will Hold Parties Accountable for Operation Chokepoint 2.0
data.description: Odaily Planet Daily News: U.S. Senator Cynthia Lummis said that today the U.S. Senate Banking Committee found the first solid evidence of Operation Chokepoint 2.0. Please rest assured that the Digital Assets Subcommittee will find the relevant parties and hold them accountable.

data.title: Raydium spent $54 million to repurchase 8.8 million RAY in January, setting a new monthly repurchase expenditure record
data.description: Odaily Planet Daily reported that data showed that Raydium spent US$54 million to repurchase 8.8 million RAY through its programmatic repurchase plan in January, setting a record high for monthly repurchase expenditure.

data.title: Analysts: Bank of England expected to resume cutting interest rates by 25 basis points
data.description: Odaily Planet Daily News The Bank of England will announce its interest rate decision tonight, and most experts predict that it will cut interest rates by 25 basis points to 4.5%, continuing a series of rate cuts that began last summer. Chris Arcari, an analyst at financial company Hymans Robertson, said that the Bank of England will have to "walk a tightrope" when it cuts interest rates further later this year. He said that although the current economy leaves room for "modest interest rate cuts", the Bank of England may take a cautious stance in the future. The rise in cost inflation is partly related to the impact of policies announced in the budget last October. British Chancellor of the Exchequer Reeves raised national insurance contributions for companies in October last year. The move is designed to allow government funds to spend more on public services, but some companies complain that this has pushed up costs and exacerbated inflation. Matthew Ryan, an analyst at financial company Ebury, added that because economic growth is stagnant but inflation is rising, the Bank of England "will have to make a judgment as to which risk may dominate this year." (Jinshi)

data.title: Japanese listed company Metaplanet's share price hits 8-year high
data.description:
Odaily Planet Daily News Market data shows that the stock price of Metaplanet, a Japanese listed company, hit an 8-year high. Metaplanet currently holds more than 1,700 bitcoins. 
Earlier in late January, Metaplanet officially announced its Bitcoin strategic plan for 2025 to 2026, hoarding 10,000 bitcoins by the end of 2025 and further increasing to 21,000 bitcoins by the end of 2026.

data.title: Bank Of England Expected To Cut Interest Rates Amid Economic Challenges
data.description:
According to Odaily, the Bank of England is set to announce its interest rate decision tonight, with most experts predicting a 25 basis point cut to 4.5%. This move would continue a series of rate reductions that began last summer. Chris Arcari, an analyst at financial firm Hymans Robertson, noted that the Bank of England will need to "walk a tightrope" when considering further rate cuts later this year. While the current economic conditions allow for "moderate rate cuts," the bank may adopt a cautious approach moving forward.

The rise in cost inflation is partly attributed to policies announced in last October's budget. UK Chancellor Reeves increased corporate national insurance contributions, aiming to allocate more government funds to public services. However, some companies have complained that this has raised costs and exacerbated inflation.

Matthew Ryan, an analyst at financial company Ebury, added that with economic growth stagnating and inflation rising, the Bank of England will need to assess which risk might dominate this year.

data.title: In the past 24 hours, the entire network has liquidated $187 million, mainly long orders, and ETH liquidation exceeds BTC
data.description:
Odaily Planet Daily News Coinglass data shows that in the past 24 hours, the entire network has been liquidated for $187 million, and a total of 89,313 people have been liquidated; among them, long orders have been liquidated for $119 million, and short orders have been liquidated for $67.9046 million.
ETH liquidated $40.3045 million, exceeding BTC (liquidated $39.0865 million).

data.title: Sen. Warren urges Trump to back CFPB to fight debanking
data.description:
Odaily Planet Daily News Democratic Senator Elizabeth Warren urged Trump to work with regulators to stop the unwarranted closure of citizens and corporate bank accounts, and believed that attempts to suppress the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB) would worsen the problem.
In a letter to Trump, Warren asked the new government to support the CFPB's efforts to prevent debanking. After firing Director Rohit Chopra, the Trump administration appointed Scott Bessent, a pro-cryptocurrency Treasury Secretary, as Acting Director. Bessent has stopped some activities of the bureau's staff.
Warren wrote before a Senate Banking Committee hearing on Wednesday that the issue involved both parties and emphasized that Trump recently publicly condemned Bank of America CEO Brian Moynihan. At the World Economic Forum in Davos, Trump accused the bank of restricting business with certain customers and accused JPMorgan CEO Jamie Dimon of doing the same to conservative customers.
“Sometimes banks have legitimate reasons and legal obligations to freeze or close bank accounts. But banks may implement these legal obligations in a sloppy and overbroad manner to cut costs,” Warren wrote in the Feb. 4 letter.
At a Senate committee hearing on Wednesday, Anchorage Digital CEO Nathan McCauley testified that he was a victim of debanking, but he declined to name the bank. McCauley said the bank told Anchorage in 2023 that it would close the company’s account because it was uncomfortable with Anchorage’s crypto customers.
“They refused to provide further explanation or allow us to speak with the risk management team, and we had great difficulty finding new banking partners,” McCauley said. “We spoke to approximately 40 banks across the country but were rejected, and many told us that they had a blanket policy prohibiting servicing crypto customers.”
A spokesperson for the Bank Policy Institute, which represents the banking industry, said in an emailed statement: “Banks must manage risks and meet regulators’ requirements, which may also limit banks’ disclosure of why accounts were closed. An important part of the solution is to fix the regulatory structure.” (Bloomberg)
